data.title: Upravnik Platform
data.excerpt: Full-stack, role-based property-management platform for residential buildings in Serbia, digitizing the relationship between building managers, board members, and residents.
data.excerpt_xl:
Upravnik Platform is a full-stack, role-based property-management system for residential buildings in Serbia. It digitizes the relationship between building managers, elected board members, and residents, replacing informal WhatsApp and Viber groups as well as paper notices with a structured, closed platform for announcements, community forums, private support tickets, real-time chat, and document sharing.

It's a solo-built project spanning a NestJS/Prisma/PostgreSQL API and a Next.js/React frontend, designed and built end-to-end, data model, permissions, business logic, and UI, all built by one engineer.

stats.label: Apps required, it works in any browser

overview.lede: Upravnik Platform is a full-stack, role-based property-management system for residential buildings in Serbia. It digitizes the relationship between building managers, elected board members, and residents, replacing informal WhatsApp and Viber groups as well as paper notices with a single, structured platform for announcements, community forums, private support tickets, real-time chat, and document sharing.

results.label: Transparency
results.value: Every announcement, ticket, and reply lives in one shared, searchable record.

results.label: Web-based
results.value: Runs entirely in the browser — no additional apps required.

results.label: All in one place
results.value: Announcements, forum, support tickets, chat, and documents

snapshot.realTime: Socket.IO-powered chat with instant read-receipts and automatic notifications
snapshot.apiSurface: 46 API endpoints across 13 feature areas, plus real-time chat
snapshot.contractDiscipline: Every API change ships with full, up-to-date documentation
snapshot.soloNote: Solo-developer project, meaning one person designed the data model, wrote the permission logic, built the API, and built the entire frontend.

problem.lede: Residential buildings in Serbia, especially larger complexes made up of multiple connected blocks, are typically run on an ad hoc basis: a building manager pins notices to a physical board, residents raise issues in a WhatsApp group, and nothing keeps a durable record of who asked for what, or whether a maintenance issue was ever actually resolved.
body: A Complex is an optional grouping of buildings that share something physical, a courtyard, a parking structure, a shared boiler room. Not every building belongs to one.
A Building always belongs to at most one Complex, and owns its own units, announcements, documents, forum threads, tickets, and chat.
A Unit (apartment, office, or commercial space) belongs to exactly one Building and can be linked to at most one account, enforced at the database level, not just in application logic.

Accounts are never self-registered, there's no public sign-up flow. A building manager provisions every resident's account and hands out the login credentials directly, mirroring the real-world process where a manager already knows who lives where. This closed model sidesteps the trust and verification problems a public registration flow would otherwise introduce.
problem.closing: This closed, hierarchical model is the foundation everything else in the system builds on, every authorization decision in the backend ultimately traces back to "which Building(s) is this user a member of, and with what role."

backend.title: Backend
backend.body: Built on NestJS with Prisma and a PostgreSQL database (containerized for consistent local and production environments), JWT-based authentication, a real-time chat layer, and auto-generated API documentation. Every incoming request is strictly validated, any field the API doesn't explicitly expect is rejected automatically, closing off a common class of security issues.

frontend.title: Frontend
frontend.body: Built with Next.js, React, and TypeScript, using role-based routing to give each type of user, residents, board members, building managers, their own tailored experience, backed by a consistent design system. Choosing the newest versions of Next.js and React was a deliberate bet on staying current with the fastest-moving parts of the ecosystem.

layers.name: Identity
layers.models: One unified account system for every person on the platform —> admins, building managers, board members, and residents alike.

layers.name: Structure
layers.models: The physical hierarchy: complexes, buildings, and individual units.

layers.name: Membership
layers.models: Role-based access control, scoped to the specific building each person belongs to, permissions never leak across buildings.

layers.name: Content
layers.models: Announcements and shared documents —> low-interaction, building-scoped content.

layers.name: Interaction
layers.models: Everything with back-and-forth conversation: forum threads, support tickets, cross-building discussions, and live chat.

layers.name: System
layers.models: Cross-cutting concerns like activity logging and notifications.

dataModel.rolesIntro: The permission system separates who someone is from what they can do. Every person is either platform staff or a resident tied to a specific unit, and separately holds a role scoped to one building: building manager, board member, or resident. A small number of platform administrators sit above the building-level roles entirely, with system-wide access. The database itself guarantees a person can only hold one role per building, so permissions can never become ambiguous.

authLayers.title: Platform-level access control
authLayers.body: The simplest layer: a single check that gates platform-wide operations, like creating new buildings or administrator accounts, to platform administrators only.

authLayers.title: Role-based permissions
authLayers.body: A declarative layer that restricts specific actions to specific roles. For example, only building managers and board members can post announcements.

authLayers.title: Ownership-aware permissions
authLayers.body: The most nuanced layer, used in the two most complex areas of the product: support tickets and cross-building discussions. Here, access depends not just on role but on ownership. A resident can view and reply to their own support ticket, while staff can act on any ticket in the building.

dataModel.callerRoleNote: The trickiest permission logic lives in the cross-building forum: since a complex can span multiple buildings, and someone might be a resident in one building but a board member in another, the system automatically grants them their highest applicable role across the whole complex, and only blocks access if they have no connection to any building in it at all.

businessLogic.title: Smart read-tracking & notifications
businessLogic.body: Opening a ticket automatically marks it as read; replying does the same. Unread status updates instantly with no extra infrastructure required. Every ticket event triggers the right notifications automatically: creating a ticket alerts building staff, a resident's reply re-notifies staff, and a staff reply notifies only the resident who opened it keeping everyone informed without notification overload. All in-app messaging is written natively in Serbian, matching the platform's real-world audience.

businessLogic.title: Reliable bulk setup
businessLogic.body: Setting up a new complex, along with all of its buildings and every individual unit, happens as a single, all-or-nothing operation. If any part fails, nothing is partially created, avoiding the mess a failed multi-step setup would otherwise leave behind. This was built specifically to power the admin bulk-setup wizard on the frontend. The backend and frontend were designed together as one feature, not built separately.

body: Every API change is fully documented as it happens, complete with example requests, responses, permission requirements, and error cases, so the frontend (or any future collaborator) always has an accurate, up-to-date reference. A running changelog tracks every change chronologically, giving a clear, readable history of how the API evolved.

Documentation is treated as a real deliverable, not an afterthought: a living design document covers the project's mission, the account and role model, the data structure, and an explicit roadmap for what gets built in what order. A complete handoff specification, covering layout, data format, and behavior, was written for the bulk-setup wizard before it was built, so the frontend and backend could be designed together as one feature.

The real build order tells its own story: start with the core structure (buildings, units, accounts, and roles), then add platform administration, then operational details, then the private ticketing system, then read receipts, then cross-building discussions. It's a steady, logical progression, get the foundation right first, then layer in product features.

frontendDeepDive.body: The frontend is split into fully separate experiences for each type of user, residents, board members, building managers, and platform administrators. Each has its own tailored navigation and layout, automatically enforced at the routing level. Role-based design goes well beyond navigation: the same page can render an entirely different view depending on who's logged in. For example, the support-tickets page shows building managers a full management view, while residents see a simplified view of just their own tickets. Data fetching and caching are handled by a single, consistent layer across the whole app, kept carefully in sync with every backend change.

standoutFeature.title: Bulk Setup Wizard
standoutFeature.intro: The single most elaborate feature in the frontend, and the clearest example of the backend and frontend being designed together as one feature.
steps: A 3-step guided wizard: Scope, Buildings, Review. This lets an administrator set up a single building, several standalone buildings, or an entire complex in one flow.
Units can be generated automatically (by floor count and units per floor, with a choice of numbering style) or entered manually, building by building.
A CSV import mode that automatically detects whether the file describes one building, several buildings, or a full complex, and flags row-level errors before anything is submitted.
A live preview that reflects the in-progress setup in real time as an easy-to-scan structure tree, so the admin can sanity-check everything before committing.
The entire structure, complex, buildings, and units are created in one reliable step, and the admin is taken straight to the new building list afterward.

featureInventory.name: Secure login with role-based access for every type of user
Unified activity feed with announcements and forum posts in one place, pinned items first
Threaded discussions, both within a building and across an entire complex
Private support tickets creation, reply to resolution, with unread indicators
Resident and unit directory with occupancy status

designSystem.intro: The interface uses a custom color system built for clarity: a teal brand color, warm neutral tones, and three accent colors used consistently to color-code content types throughout the app. Announcements, forum, chat, and documents each have their own recognizable color. Typography pairs a clean UI typeface with a monospace font for numbers and timestamps.

warStory.title: The sidebar bug
warStory.body: During a particularly dense feature push, shipping the ticket system, color-coded categories, and the bulk setup wizard all at once, a real bug turned up in the building navigation sidebar: expanding a building would immediately collapse again, making it impossible to browse between buildings. The fix was small and targeted: track which building was previously active, and only reset its expand/collapse state when that actually changes.

scopeDeferrals.title: Real-time chat interface
scopeDeferrals.note: The backend fully supports secure, real-time messaging per building, but the frontend doesn't have a chat interface yet — that page currently shows a "coming soon" placeholder.

scopeDeferrals.title: Full document management
scopeDeferrals.note: Documents can be listed and linked to today; uploading and previewing files directly in the app is still on the roadmap.

scopeDeferrals.title: Building manager dashboard widgets
scopeDeferrals.note: Pinned content, personal to-dos, and an activity feed are planned but not yet built — currently shown as a roadmap preview.

backend.detail: Core data structure: accounts, complexes, buildings, units, membership, announcements, documents, forums, chat, activity logs, and notifications
Introduced a platform-level administrator role, separate from building-level roles
Added operational details like resident counts per unit
Added unread indicators for support tickets
Added discussions that span an entire complex, not just a single building

frontend.detail: One large push laying down nearly the entire app: role-based navigation for every user type, core pages, the full component library, and the data-fetching layer

A dense follow-up push that shipped the entire support-ticket system end-to-end, introduced color-coded content categories, fixed a sidebar navigation bug, added a mobile account menu, and built the full bulk setup wizard including CSV import and live preview

data.title: STK Vojvodina, Novi Sad
data.excerpt: STK Vojvodina is one of Serbia's most historic table tennis clubs, known for decades of competitive success and youth development in Novi Sad.
data.excerpt_xl:
STK Vojvodina is a table tennis club based in Novi Sad, founded in the mid-20th century, with a track record of competing at the highest domestic levels and a long-running youth development program.

The site brings that together in one place — club history, competition results, youth programs, and news updates — organized into a structure that stays easy to maintain as the club adds new content.

Performance, accessibility, and responsive design were treated as requirements, not afterthoughts, since the audience ranges from players and parents checking results on a phone to supporters and partners browsing on a desktop.

data.title: World Best Brands
data.excerpt: Distribution company based in Belgrade, Serbia, specializing in import and export of FMCG products from top global brands.
data.excerpt_xl: World Best Brands LLC is a distribution and trading company headquartered in Belgrade, Serbia, founded in August 2020. It imports and exports fast-moving consumer goods — groceries, toiletries, household, and laundry items — carrying products from brands including Coca-Cola, Mars, Jacobs, Ferrero, Red Bull, Milka, Procter & Gamble, and Unilever, alongside its own private label line.

data.title: Dr Bean Coffee
data.excerpt: Small workshop based in Novi Sad, specializing in fresh Turkish and espresso coffee.
data.excerpt_xl: Small coffee workshop in Novi Sad, roasting Turkish and espresso coffee from Arabica beans sourced from Brazil's Minas Gerais region, sold freshly roasted for home use.

data.title: Advokat u Novom Sadu
data.excerpt: Law office representing clients across contract, labor, criminal, and other areas of law, before all courts, government bodies, and institutions.
data.excerpt_xl: Advokatska Kancelarija, in Novi Sad, is a legal practice led by attorneys Dijana Biro, Davor Marić, and Milan Nedić, covering contract law, tort law, labor law, commercial law, property and civil law, criminal and misdemeanor law, administrative law, debt collection, and representation in public auctions. The attorneys represent clients before courts of all jurisdictions as well as other state bodies and institutions.

data.title: Pelemiš Dental Care
data.excerpt: Dental clinic in Novi Sad providing check-ups, cleanings, treatments, and oral surgeries.
data.excerpt_xl: Pelemiš Dental Care is a dental clinic in Novi Sad offering routine check-ups, dental cleanings, treatments, and oral surgeries.
